body_txt: Rock stars often get so into the hardness of their music that they actually destroy instruments. This most often takes the form of smashing a guitar (hence the trope name) on the stage as hard as possible, pounding it to fragments with a few whacks. Some rock bands set their instruments on fire. The drums and other instruments might get smashed, too. Trent Reznor would even do this with Yamaha [ DX7 ] synthesizers. And these aren't fictional works trying to make rock seem bad . This is Truth in Television . So much so that it has its own page on the other wiki. While once a subversive, fittingly rebellious practice, it's arguably become a Dead Horse Trope , and it's not without controversy. It can definitely seem like a waste of a perfectly playable instrument, and sometimes, it is. It also may be a great way to tick off your equipment sponsors, who might even provide you with instruments on tour just so you don't have to worry about your beloved axe being smashed by TSA. This act might also not sit right with some fans, recognizing that not everyone is able to afford the "cheap" guitar that you just wasted. However, there may be hope for smashed guitars. A luthier, or guitar repairperson, may be able to fix a shattered instrument with some glue, new parts, and TLC , though this is more commonly done for accidentally destroyed instruments that have sentimental or monetary value. Also, many electric guitars have bolt-on necks, meaning if the rocker only destroys the neck or headstock, repairs are as simple as buying and screwing on a new neck. Smashing a solid-bodied electric guitar is much harder than you'd expect from seeing this trope in fiction. It often takes several tries on a well-built instrument before it becomes unplayable. Many a young guitarist has gotten frustrated with his instrument in the middle of a show and decided to smash it , only to find out that his instrument is Made of Iron (or rather, hardwood). When the instrument destruction is more planned and theatrical, bands will often rig the instrument to smash apart easily in order to please the audience. Or musicians will deliberately buy extremely cheap junk instruments so they can justify smashing them. At least one anecdotal story details a country-rock star who was challenged in a letter from a little boy too poor to buy his own guitar, saying "If you don't want it, why not give it away?" That musician has done so ever since. Compare Great Balls of Fire! , Can-Crushing Cranium , Dramatic Shattering . At times they can push it further with Trash the Set as well. See also Piano Drop for more musical instrument destruction, and All Drummers Are Animals for more rock musicians behaving wildly. Despite the name, this is not limited to guitars or to rock music.
